Contents
How to Use This Guide
When you read for pleasure, you let the story wash over you. That's wonderful, b...
To learn from the books you love, you need to read differently. You need to noti...
This guide will help you do that. It gives you a framework for tracking the elem...
What Is "Craft" Anyway?
You'll see the word "craft" throughout this guide. Let's be clear about what it ...
Craft is the HOW of storytelling. It's not WHAT happens in your story—it's how y...

Craft Includes

    Structure: How the story is organized. Where beats fall. How...
Structure: How the story is organized. Where beats fall. How chapters begin and ...
Scene-Level Choices: What's shown vs. told. Whose POV we're in. Where the scene ...
Line-Level Choices: Word selection. Sentence rhythm. Dialogue patterns. How desc...
Emotional Engineering: How the author makes you feel things. The buildup before ...
When we talk about "craft moves," we mean specific, replicable techniques. Not "...
The goal of this guide is to help you see these moves—to make the invisible visi...

The Goal
    When you can look at a page and see not just a dragon, but a "Catal...
Choose Your Analysis Level
You don't have to fill in every section. Choose the level that fits your reading...

Quick-Fill
    About 2-3 minutes per chapter, plus 20 minutes when you finish th...
About 2-3 minutes per chapter, plus 20 minutes when you finish the book.
You'll complete chapter logs with tension ratings, do a quick post-read synthesi...
Ideal if you're reading for pleasure but want to stay aware of craft.

Deep-Dive
    About 5-10 minutes per chapter, plus an hour or more after finishi...
About 5-10 minutes per chapter, plus an hour or more after finishing.
Fill in every question in the chapter logs — you can ignore the QF labels and wo...
Ideal if you're doing serious craft study and want to understand how the book wo...

If you're reading a physical book, colored sticky page markers or highlighters c...

Key Terms
You'll see these terms throughout your analysis. Knowing what they mean will hel...
Essential Vocabulary
Adhesion: This is the external force that keeps characters together when they'd ...
The Wound: The past trauma that shapes how your character sees love and the worl...
The Lie: A false belief the character holds that must be overcome for them to gr...
Deep POV: A narrative perspective that puts the reader inside the character's he...
Slow Burn: A pacing strategy that builds romantic tension through delayed gratif...
HEA/HFN: Happily Ever After or Happy For Now. Romance readers expect one of thes...
Dual-Arc Vocabulary
Through-Thread: The causal chain that makes romance and fantasy arcs inseparable...
Double Helix: A structural model where romance and fantasy arcs twist around eac...
The Bridge: The specific mechanism that binds two plots together: a magical bond...
Midpoint Rupture: The moment (usually near 50%) where both arcs collide and tran...
Emotional Mirror: When the state of the world reflects the state of the relation...
Bolted-On Romance: A structural pitfall where the romance could be removed witho...
Pre-Read Setup
Before you start reading, take a few minutes to record what you expect from this...

Expectations & Goals
What do I expect from this book based on cover/blurb?
What specific craft element am I hoping to learn from this book?
What weakness in my own writing do I hope this book can help me improve?
The Craft Toolkit
Before you start logging chapters, familiarize yourself with these core craft co...
Story Structure: The Big Picture
Every story is a series of promises and payoffs. Structure is how you organize t...

The Three-Act Framework
    Act 1 (0-25%): Setup. Establish the normal world, in...
Act 1 (0-25%): Setup. Establish the normal world, introduce the protagonist's wo...
Act 2 (25-75%): Confrontation. Escalating obstacles, a midpoint shift (false vic...
Act 3 (75-100%): Resolution. The protagonist faces their deepest fear, makes a c...

Key Story Beats to Recognize
    The Hook: First pages—creates a question the re...
The Hook: First pages—creates a question the reader needs answered.
Inciting Incident: The event that starts the story engine. Around 10-12%.
First Plot Point: Protagonist commits to the journey. Around 25%.
Midpoint: Major shift—false victory, defeat, or revelation. 50%.
All Is Lost: Rock bottom. The protagonist's worst fear realized. Around 75%.
Climax: Final confrontation. Everything the story promised comes due. 90-95%.
Resolution: New normal established. Final image mirrors or contrasts opening.
Scene Craft: The Building Blocks
Stories are made of scenes. A scene is a unit of story that happens in one time ...

What Every Scene Needs
    A Character With a Goal: Someone wants something in t...
A Character With a Goal: Someone wants something in this scene. If no one wants ...
Obstacles: Something prevents the character from getting what they want easily. ...
Stakes: Why does achieving (or failing to achieve) this goal matter? What's at r...
Change: Something must be different at the end of the scene than at the beginnin...

Scene-Level Techniques
    Scene Entry: Start as late as possible. Skip the arri...
Scene Entry: Start as late as possible. Skip the arrival, skip the small talk, s...
Scene Exit: End as early as possible—often before the scene "finishes." Cut on t...
Scene Sequels: After an action scene, characters need time to react, process, an...
Scene Types: Action scenes (something happens), reaction scenes (characters proc...
Tension & Pacing: The Rhythm
Pacing isn't just "fast" or "slow"—it's the rhythm of tension and release that k...

Creating Tension
    Questions: Every unanswered question creates tension. Who i...
Questions: Every unanswered question creates tension. Who is the masked figure? ...
Dramatic Irony: When readers know something characters don't—massive tension eng...
Ticking Clocks: Deadlines create urgency. The ball ends at midnight. The poison ...
Competing Desires: When a character wants two things that are mutually exclusive...

Controlling Pace
    Speed Up: Short sentences. Short paragraphs. Action verbs. ...
Speed Up: Short sentences. Short paragraphs. Action verbs. Dialogue without tags...
Slow Down: Longer sentences. Sensory details. Internal thought. Description. Ref...
Chapter Length: Short chapters = fast pace, hard to put down. Long chapters = im...
Scene Breaks: White space signals time passing, perspective shifts, or tonal cha...
Character Craft: Making People Real
Characters aren't just descriptions—they're patterns of behavior, contradiction,...

Character Essentials
    The Want (External Goal): What the character is activel...
The Want (External Goal): What the character is actively pursuing. Defeat the vi...
The Need (Internal Goal): What the character actually needs to grow, usually som...
The Wound: Past pain that created a false belief about the world. "Love leads to...
The Flaw: Behavioral manifestation of the wound. Pushes people away, trusts no o...

Showing Character
    Action Under Pressure: Who someone is gets revealed when s...
Action Under Pressure: Who someone is gets revealed when stakes are high. Easy c...
Contradiction: Real people are inconsistent. A killer who's gentle with animals....
Specificity: Not "she was nervous" but "she kept touching the scar on her wrist....
Voice: How a character speaks—word choice, rhythm, what they notice—should be di...
Line-Level Craft: The Micro Skills
Big-picture structure matters, but stories live or die at the sentence level. Th...

Sentence Techniques
    Vary Length: Long sentences for flow and immersion. Shor...
Vary Length: Long sentences for flow and immersion. Short sentences for impact. ...
End on Strength: The most important word should come last. Not "She was dead, he...
Active Voice: "She drew her sword" not "The sword was drawn by her." Passive voi...
Concrete Over Abstract: "Grief" is abstract. "She kept setting two places at the...

Description & Detail
    The Telling Detail: One perfect detail beats five gener...
The Telling Detail: One perfect detail beats five generic ones. Not "the room wa...
Character-Filtered: Description should reveal who's observing. A soldier notices...
In Motion: Static description stops story. Weave details into action. She notice...
Sensory Balance: Most writers default to visual. Use sound, smell, touch, taste....
Dialogue Craft: Making Characters Talk
Great dialogue does multiple jobs at once—reveals character, advances plot, crea...

Dialogue Principles
    Subtext: Characters rarely say what they mean. "Fine" me...
Subtext: Characters rarely say what they mean. "Fine" means "I'm furious." "What...
Agenda: Every character in a conversation wants something, even if it's just to ...
Distinct Voices: Cover the dialogue tags. Can you tell who's speaking? Vocabular...
Compression: Real speech is boring. Fictional dialogue cuts the boring parts. No...

Dialogue Techniques
    Misdirection: Answer a different question than was asked...
Misdirection: Answer a different question than was asked. Creates tension, revea...
Interruption: Characters cutting each other off shows conflict, urgency, power d...
Silence: What's NOT said matters. "She didn't answer" can be more powerful than ...
Beats: Small actions between dialogue lines. She turned away. He set down the cu...

The Goal of This Guide
    You now have the vocabulary. As you read, you'll star...
Chapter-by-Chapter Logs
These logs help you capture observations while they're fresh. Fill one out after...
How to Use These Chapter Logs
Reading like a writer means noticing things. But noticing without a framework ju...

What to Track in Every Chapter
    The Chapter's Job: What does this chapter DO ...
The Chapter's Job: What does this chapter DO for the story? Advance the plot? De...
Opening & Closing: Pay special attention to the first and last paragraphs. How d...
The Emotional Shift: Characters should feel different at the end of a chapter th...
Tension Types: Romantic tension and plot tension often move in opposite directio...

Specific Things to Notice
    For Romance: Any moment of awareness between the c...
For Romance: Any moment of awareness between the characters? Physical proximity?...
For Fantasy Plot: What new information do we get? What questions are raised? Wha...
For Craft: Did any sentence stop you? Any technique you want to steal? Great dia...
For Pacing: Did the chapter feel too long, too short, or just right? Was there a...
Many writers find it helpful to do two passes. First pass: read for pure enjoyme...
Chapter Logs
Fill these out immediately after finishing each chapter. Your first impressions ...

Romance Arc Tracking
Romance readers have expectations for how a love story should unfold, even if th...
Romancing the Beat Framework
This framework comes from Gwen Hayes's book Romancing the Beat and breaks the ro...
As you read, look for these beats. Not every book will hit them all, and some wi...
In romantasy specifically, these romance beats must weave seamlessly with fantas...

The Romance Promise
    Romance is the only genre with a mandatory ending. Reade...

Use the scale below for consistent ratings across all the scenes you log.
Heat Level Scale
0 – Clean: No physical intimacy beyond hand-holding
1 – Sweet: Closed-door, sweet kisses
2 – Mild: Heated kissing, some touching
3 – Moderate: Fade to black with buildup
4 – Steamy: On-page intimacy, some detail
5 – Explicit: Detailed, explicit scenes
